serving.natural_language_portion: A standard single-plate serving of pork chop baked rice is approximately 350 g, comprising a base of white rice topped with one pork chop, cream sauce, and melted cheese, as typically served in Singapore coffee shops and casual restaurants.

nutrient_source.title: Pork Chop Baked Rice — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Pork Chop Baked Rice (cr_id F05100105). All core macros and secondary nutrients are taken from this canonical entry with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated at approximately 2.2 g/100 g based on typical pork chop and cheese composition per USDA SR Legacy as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this entry.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pork chop baked rice as a composite dish. GI estimated at 72 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: white rice (jasmine/long-grain), which has a published GI of approximately 72 (University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al., Diabetes Care 2008). Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (72 × 18.7 × 3.5) / 100 ≈ 23.7 GL units for a 350 g serving. The cream sauce, cheese, and pork chop components contribute fat and protein which modestly attenuate the glycaemic response, but the white rice base dominates the GI profile.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium at 357 mg/100 g; borderline level with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to reduce total sodium intake, request reduced cheese and sauce quantities, and avoid adding additional salt or soy sauce, as a standard 350 g serving delivers approximately 1,250 mg sodium — over half the recommended daily limit of 2,000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice base (GI ~72) with carbohydrate 18.7 g/100 g and low fibre-to-carb ratio
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit this dish due to its high-GI white rice base and estimated glycemic load of approximately 23.7 per serving; substituting brown rice or cauliflower rice and reducing portion size are recommended strategies to lower postprandial blood glucose response.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from pork chop and melted cheese (~2.2 g/100 g) combined with moderate sodium (357 mg/100 g)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish sparingly, as the combination of saturated fat from pork and cheese and borderline sodium content may adversely affect LDL cholesterol and blood pressure; requesting reduced cheese topping and a smaller portion is advisable.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Arachidonic acid from pork (red meat) and potential nightshade sensitivity from tomato-based sauce
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid this dish or substitute the pork chop with chicken or fish to reduce arachidonic acid (omega-6) intake, and should monitor their response to tomato-based sauce as a nightshade-derived ingredient that may exacerbate inflammation in sensitive individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pork Chop Baked Rice (猪扒焗饭, Zhu Pa Ju Fan) is a popular Hong Kong-style Western comfort dish widely served in Singapore coffee shops, casual restaurants, and cha chaan teng-style eateries. The dish consists of a base of steamed white rice topped with a pan-fried or baked pork chop, covered with a creamy tomato-based sauce and finished with a generous layer of melted cheese, then baked in an oven until the cheese is golden and bubbling. The result is a hearty, rich dish with a crispy cheese crust, tender pork, and a savoury-creamy sauce that permeates the rice. Nutritionally, per 100 g it provides 158 kcal, 8.9 g protein, 5.26 g fat, and 18.7 g carbohydrate, with 357 mg sodium and 68 mg calcium. The white rice base gives the dish a high glycemic index (estimated GI ~72), making it a high-glycemic-load meal unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes without portion modification. The sodium content at 357 mg/100 g is borderline for hypertension management, and the saturated fat from pork and cheese warrants caution for cardiovascular health. The dish is not halal due to pork content and contains dairy allergens from the cheese and cream sauce. It is a culturally significant dish in Singapore's food landscape, reflecting the local adaptation of Hong Kong-style Western cuisine.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100105) with confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork and cheese composition data as HPB does not separately report this field. GI estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using white rice as the dominant carbohydrate analogue, with clear documentation of the estimation basis.
notes: sugar_g is reported as 0.0 g/100 g per HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB Foodo database entry — the cream sauce and tomato base contribute negligible free sugars at this preparation level. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed data for this composite baked rice dish are not separately reported in the HPB Foodo database as of 2026, and no sufficiently close composite entry exists in USDA FoodData Central to substitute these values without unacceptable estimation error: monounsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fraction not reported by HPB for this dish; polyunsaturated_fat_g — fatty acid fraction not reported by HPB for this dish; trans_fat_g — trans fat fraction not reported by HPB for this dish; potassium_mg — mineral not reported by HPB for this composite dish; cholesterol_mg — not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_c_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_a_mcg_rae — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b3_niacin_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b6_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_b12_mcg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; folate_mcg_dfe — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_d_mcg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; vitamin_e_mg — micronutrient not reported by HPB for this composite dish; phosphorus_mg — mineral not reported by HPB for this composite dish; magnesium_mg — mineral not reported by HPB for this composite dish; zinc_mg — mineral not reported by HPB for this composite dish; selenium_mcg — trace element not reported by HPB for this composite dish; water_g — moisture content not reported by HPB for this composite dish. Sources consulted that confirmed unavailability: HPB Singapore Foodo database (https://foodo.hpb.gov.sg/, accessed 2026-04-16) and USDA FoodData Central (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/, accessed 2026-04-16). Saturated fat estimated at 2.2 g/100 g using USDA SR Legacy data for pork loin chop (cooked) and cheddar cheese in proportions consistent with the dish description; this is an approximation and should be replaced with a direct HPB measurement when available. GI of 72 is an analogous estimate based on white rice (jasmine) from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this composite dish was found in approved sources. The dish contains pork and is therefore not halal. Allergens include milk (cream, cheese) and may include gluten depending on the flour used in the cream sauce preparation.
